column4: El presides over the divine council. Yam (Sea) is named as king and demands Baal’s surrender. The gods are fearful. Baal is reluctant but is encouraged by Anat and Astarte.
column6: El, Yam, Baal, Anat, Astarte
column7: El’s decree (1.1 IV–V); Yam’s messengers; Baal’s initial hesitation.

column4: Baal, armed by Kothar-wa-Khasis, defeats Yam in a cosmic battle. Baal is established as a champion of the gods.
column6: Baal, Yam, Kothar-wa-Khasis
column7: Baal’s victory (1.2 II–IV); weapons motif; Baal’s ascendance.

column4: Baal seeks a palace to cement his kingship. El refuses. Anat and Athirat intercede, offering gifts and persuasion. El eventually consents.
column6: Baal, El, Anat, Athirat
column7: Baal’s petition (1.3 III–IV); Athirat’s mediation.

column4: Kothar-wa-Khasis builds Baal’s palace, initially without windows. Baal insists on opening them, showing confidence. Baal hosts a feast, marking his enthronement.
column6: Baal, Kothar-wa-Khasis, El, Anat
column7: Palace built (1.4 V–VII); feast (1.4 VIII–IX).

column4: Baal sends messengers to Mot (Death), inviting him to the feast. Mot threatens Baal. Baal descends to the underworld and is overcome by Mot. Drought and infertility afflict the earth.
column6: Baal, Mot, messengers
column7: Baal’s descent (1.5); Mot’s threats; land suffers.

column4: Anat mourns Baal, confronts Mot, and violently slays him. Baal is resurrected and returns to his throne. Mot revives and challenges Baal again, but Baal refuses further conflict. Mot submits. Cosmic and agricultural order is restored.
column6: Anat, Baal, Mot
column7: Anat’s violence (1.6 I–II); Baal’s return (1.6 III–V); Mot’s submission.

column4: The cycle closes with a hymn or disputation poem, celebrating Baal’s restored reign and the return of fertility and order.
column6: Baal, Mot, gods
column7: Hymn/disputation (1.6 VI–VII).

column2: Baal (storm god) battles Yam (Sea), defeats him, and is enthroned as king (KTU 1.1–1.2)12.
column3: Storm god (e.g., Indra, Zeus, Thor) slays serpent/dragon (Vṛtra, Typhon, Jörmungandr).
column4: Yahweh defeats sea/chaos monsters (Leviathan, Rahab) in Psalms 74:13-14, 89:9-10; Isaiah 27:1; Job 26:12-13.
column5: Marduk (storm god) battles and kills Tiamat (Sea/Chaos), then is enthroned as king241011.

column2: Yam and Nahar (Sea and River) represent primordial chaos, opposed by Baal12.
column3: Serpent/dragon often represents primordial chaos; storm god brings order.
column4: Genesis 1:2 “the deep” (tehom, related to Tiamat); chaos subdued at creation.
column5: Apsu (fresh water) and Tiamat (salt water) are primordial chaos; Tiamat’s defeat brings order and creation6101112.

column2: Asherah (Athirat), “Lady of the Sea,” mother of gods, negotiates for Baal2.
column3: Earth or water goddesses (Danu, Jord, Gaia) as primordial mothers.
column4: Eve, Wisdom (Proverbs), and the sea as a feminine symbol; not as prominent as in myth.
column5: Tiamat is the primordial mother, “she who bore them all,” and embodiment of the sea2710.

column2: Kothar-wa-Khasis forges weapons for Baal to defeat Yam2.
column3: Divine weapons forged for storm gods (e.g., Indra’s vajra, Thor’s Mjolnir, Zeus’s thunderbolt).
column4: Yahweh’s “mighty arm” (Exod 15:6); angelic hosts as agents.
column5: Marduk receives special weapons, including winds and a net, to defeat Tiamat210.

column2: Baal’s kingship is challenged; other gods (Athtar) fail to take his place, affirming Baal’s unique role1.
column3: Zeus succeeds Cronus; Indra’s repeated contests; Odin’s rule challenged.
column4: Yahweh’s unique kingship (Psalms 47, 93, 97); Daniel 7 (Son of Man enthroned).
column5: Marduk is proclaimed king of the gods after defeating Tiamat and is given supreme authority11011.

column2: Baal’s palace is built after his victory, symbolizing kingship and cosmic order (KTU 1.3–1.4)12.
column3: Zeus builds Olympus; Indra builds his palace; Norse gods build Asgard.
column4: Yahweh’s temple built after victory (Exodus, 1 Kings 8); Psalm 29 (Yahweh enthroned over the flood).
column5: Marduk builds Babylon and its temple (Esagila) after his victory10.

column2: Baal descends to the underworld after challenge by Mot, dies, mourned by Anat, and returns to life (KTU 1.5–1.6)1.
column3: Indra “swallowed” by Vṛtra and released; Osiris dies and is resurrected; Baldr’s death and foretold return.
column4: Jesus’ death and resurrection (NT Gospels); Jonah in the fish (Jonah 2); resurrection themes in Ezekiel 37.
column5: Tiamat is slain, but there is no resurrection; her body becomes the cosmos. Marduk’s victory brings new order, not rebirth10.

column2: Baal hosts a feast in his new palace; gods celebrate his enthronement2.
column3: Gods feast after victory (e.g., Greek Olympian banquets).
column4: Feasts after victory (Exodus 15; 1 Kings 8 dedication of the temple).
column5: Marduk hosts a feast; gods celebrate and proclaim his names in assembly after Tiamat’s defeat210.

column2: Order is established after Baal’s victory, but not a full creation myth3.
column3: Order/civilization emerges after chaos is defeated (Greek, Norse, Vedic).
column4: Genesis 1: God creates by subduing chaos; Psalm 104; Isaiah 51:9-10 (creation and chaos subdued).
column5: Marduk creates the world from Tiamat’s corpse, divides the waters, creates humans from Kingu’s blood, and establishes Babylon4101112.

column2: Baal’s death brings drought; his return brings rain and fertility1.
column3: Indra releases waters after slaying Vṛtra; Demeter/Persephone cycle; Osiris and Nile flooding.
column4: Elijah and drought (1 Kings 17–18); agricultural festivals (Lev 23); resurrection and new life themes.
column5: Not explicit; Tiamat’s death brings cosmic order, but not directly tied to agricultural cycles10.

column3: Storm god brings order to cosmos, enabling civilization.

column4: Details & Sources

column2: Alexander the Great’s campaigns in Central and South Asia
column4: Alexander invades Persia (c. 334 BCE), Bactria, Sogdiana, and reaches Punjab (Battle of the Hydaspes, 326 BCE). Establishes Greek satrapies and cities, leaving a Hellenistic legacy in Bactria and northwest India.

column2: Greco-Bactrian Kingdom, Indo-Greek Kingdoms
column4: Successor states to Alexander’s empire, with Greek rulers in Bactria and northwest India (e.g., Menander I). Greek coins, art, and inscriptions found in modern Afghanistan, Pakistan, and India.

column2: Ptolemaic Egypt’s Red Sea trade with India
column4: Ptolemaic dynasty (Egypt) establishes Red Sea ports (e.g., Berenice, Myos Hormos) for trade with India, using monsoon winds. Greek and Indian merchants, and Indian communities in Alexandria256.

column2: Roman conquest of Egypt (30 BCE)
column4: Augustus annexes Egypt, inherits Ptolemaic trade networks. Roman direct maritime trade with India begins, using Red Sea ports12367.

column2: Embassies and direct contact between Rome and India
column4: Indian embassies to Augustus (26 and 20 BCE); Roman embassies and merchants in Indian ports (Muziris, Arikamedu, Barygaza)25. Roman coins, amphorae, and goods found in India; Indian goods (spices, gems, textiles) imported to Rome.

column2: Peak Indo-Roman trade
column4: Up to 120 Roman ships annually sail from Myos Hormos to India (Strabo). Major ports: Muziris, Arikamedu, Barbaricum, Barygaza. Roman trading settlements, temples (e.g., to Augustus at Muziris), and embassies in India. Tamil Sangam literature and the Periplus Maris Erythraei document trade and Roman merchant communities12567.

column2: Overland trade and Silk Road contacts
column4: Roman goods reach China via Silk Road intermediaries (Parthians, Kushans). Chinese records note Roman embassies (as “Daqin”) arriving in 166 CE. Silk, spices, and luxury goods flow west; Roman coins found in Southeast Asia and China8.

column2: Decline of Roman trade with India
column4: Political instability, Parthian and Sassanian control of trade routes, and crises in the Roman Empire reduce direct trade. Some Roman settlements in India persist after the fall of the Western Empire123.

column2: Byzantine and later Roman contacts with India and China
column4: Byzantine Empire continues some trade with India and China (notably for silk). Silk Road and maritime routes remain active, though less dominated by Mediterranean powers.
